unit.name: Evaluating faithfulness and systematicity in reasoning
unit.description: Presents skepticism about apparent reasoning by testing whether rationales causally matter and whether performance survives out-of-distribution changes.
unit.summary: After showing ways to induce reasoning-like behavior, the lecture asks whether the resulting rationales are faithful, meaning whether the model's answer actually depends on the rationale it produced [ts=1252s][ts=1276s]. Two diagnostic experiments are described: early exit, where the rationale is cut off after one or more sentences before forcing an answer, and rationale corruption, where mistakes are inserted partway through the rationale; when accuracy barely changes, the rationale is interpreted as a post-hoc explanation rather than a causal computation trace [ts=1306s][ts=1477s]. The lecture then proposes counterfactual or out-of-distribution evaluation by changing the setting while preserving the abstract skill, such as moving from base-10 arithmetic to base-9 arithmetic or changing world facts in logic problems, and reports sizable performance drops even on simple logic tasks [ts=1517s][ts=1642s]. A final example on analogical reasoning shows that changing the alphabet or the transformation rule sharply reduces model performance while humans degrade very little, leading to the cautious conclusion that current systems show some reasoning and some memorization but not robust systematic generalization [ts=1662s][ts=1782s].

key_points.text: A rationale is faithful only if the model's final answer actually depends on that rationale.
Early-exit testing forces the model to answer after only part of its rationale; unchanged accuracy suggests the omitted steps were not doing real work.
Corrupting an intermediate reasoning step and observing little answer change is another sign that the rationale may be post-hoc.
Base-9 arithmetic and altered-logic worlds are used as out-of-distribution tests for whether the model learned the underlying rule rather than memorized training patterns.
Analogical reasoning experiments show large drops for models under task or alphabet changes, while humans show little decrease.

Two diagnostic experiments are described: early exit, where the rationale is cut off after one or more sentences before forcing an answer, and rationale corruption, where mistakes are inserted partway through the rationale; when accuracy barely changes, the rationale is interpreted as a post-hoc explanation rather than a causal computation trace [ts=1306s][ts=1477s]. The lecture then proposes counterfactual or out-of-distribution evaluation by changing the setting while preserving the abstract skill, such as moving from base-10 arithmetic to base-9 arithmetic or changing world facts in logic problems, and reports sizable performance drops even on simple logic tasks [ts=1517s][ts=1642s]. A final example on analogical reasoning shows that changing the alphabet or the transformation rule sharply reduces model performance while humans degrade very little, leading to the cautious conclusion that current systems show some reasoning and some memorization but not robust systematic generalization [ts=1662s][ts=1782s].

kp_catalog.name: Faithfulness and counterfactual evaluation of reasoning
kp_catalog.description: Reasoning quality should be tested by whether rationales causally affect answers and whether performance survives distribution shifts.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because faithfulness and counterfactual evaluation of reasoning clarifies reasoning quality should be tested by whether rationales causally affect answers and whether performance survives distribution shifts, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Chain-of-thought prompting
kp_catalog.description: A prompting strategy that asks the model to generate intermediate reasoning before answering, either via exemplars or zero-shot prompting. Prompting with worked intermediate reasoning steps so the model generates a scratchpad before the final answer.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because chain-of-thought prompting formalizes how a prompting strategy that asks the model to generate intermediate reasoning before answering, either via exemplars or zero-shot prompting, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between capstone and gateway and importance labels split between critical and high.
